title: Pastéis de nata
subtitle: Portuguese custard tarts with charred, blistered tops
excerpt: Small Portuguese custard tarts in flaky pastry cups, baked at fierce heat until the custard is charred and blistered in patches and the pastry is shattering crisp. Best eaten hot with a cup of coffee.
sourceNotes: Pastéis de nata originate in the Jerónimos Monastery in Belém, Lisbon, where monks are documented as having made the custard tarts from the eighteenth century. The recipe was sold to a nearby sugar refinery in 1837 and the pastry became commercialised as Pastéis de Belém. The dish is public domain; the technique is documented across Portuguese culinary literature and in food-history accounts of the Belém monastery tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry cases can be formed and refrigerated overnight. Make the custard on the day of baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: as hot as the oven will go — fan or conventional maximum. An oven thermometer matters here.

glossaryTerms.definition: A shortcut puff pastry made by incorporating cold butter in larger pieces than shortcrust, then folding the dough several times. It produces flaky layers without the precision of full puff.

content.text: Pastéis de nata depend on two things: a very hot oven and a good pastry. The custard filling is simple: eggs, sugar, flour, and cream. The oven must be running at 250°C or above to char the custard in patches and blister the pastry into the shattering-crisp shells that make the tart worth eating. An oven running 30°C low produces pale, soft cups with no character. An oven thermometer is not optional here.

made by rolling and folding the dough three times to build flaky layers. The dough is then rolled tightly into a log, sliced into rounds, and each round is pressed into a deep muffin tin with thumb and forefinger to form a thin cup that rises and flakes as it bakes.

attrs.prepNote: very cold, grated or cut into small pieces, for the pastry
a small pinch, for the pastry
split and scraped, for the custard

content.text: Mix {{plain-flour}} (pastry quantity) and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add the cold {{unsalted-butter}} and rub briefly — the pieces should remain visible and pea-sized. Add 80–90 ml of cold water and mix to a rough, shaggy dough; do not overwork it. On a floured surface, roll to a rectangle, fold in thirds, rotate 90 degrees, and roll again. Repeat twice more. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Roll the chilled pastry to a thin rectangle about 3 mm thick. Roll it up tightly from the short end into a log. Slice the log into twelve 1.5 cm rounds. Press each round into a deep muffin tin cup: start at the base and work up the sides, pressing the pastry as thin as possible. The pastry should reach the rim of each cup. Refrigerate for 30 minutes while you make the custard.

Whisk the custard {{plain-flour}} with a little of the {{whole-milk}} to a paste. Add {{caster-sugar}}, the remaining {{whole-milk}}, {{double-cream}}, {{egg-yolks}}, and the seeds from {{vanilla-pod}}. Cook over medium heat, whisking constantly, until the custard just thickens and begins to bubble. Remove from the heat and cool slightly. Heat the oven to its maximum — ideally 250°C fan or above. Fill each pastry cup three-quarters full with custard. Bake for 12–15 minutes until the custard has charred patches and the pastry is deep golden. Cool for 10 minutes, then remove from the tin. Serve warm, dusted with cinnamon if you like.
The charred spots are not burnt. They are a feature: the caramelised sugar and egg are what make the authentic version taste different from a plain custard tart.
If the oven does not reach 250°C, preheat a heavy baking tray in the oven and place the muffin tin on it; the extra heat from below helps crisp the base.
Pastéis de nata should be eaten warm, not cold from the fridge. Reheat at 200°C for four minutes if making ahead.
